name: Czech A1 Vocabulary
description: Beginner Czech. Greetings, numbers, basic household vocabulary. Enough to get through introductions.

facts.explanation: tak — so (very). Also: so (therefore), so, in that way
den — day (24 hours, usually from midnight to midnight). Also: daytime (time between sunrise and sunset), day (rotational period of a body orbiting a star)
té — The name of the Latin script letter T/t.. Also: tea
vlastní — own. Also: proper (as in "proper subset" or "proper noun"), itself
muž — man (an adult male human). Also: husband
paní — Mrs. Also: woman, lady, wife
příliš — too (more or less than held optimal).
řada — row (line of objects). Also: series, succession, series, rank
velký — big. Also: major (the larger of two intervals denoted by the same ordinal number)
zákon — law (rule). Also: act (a product of a legislative body, a statute)
čas — time. Also: time (particular occasion), tense
dobrý — good. Also: the third best mark in the 5-grade scale at Czech schools
hodně — very; to a great degree or extent. Also: much; many, sufficiently, plenty; sufficient; enough
velmi — very. Also: lot, a lot
cesta — road. Also: journey, path (graph theory)
jméno — name. Also: a nominal, more commonly a noun or adjective, but also a pronoun or a numeral
pohled — look (facial expression). Also: look, glance, gaze, stare, viewpoint, angle, perspective (opinion), postcard
případ — case (one of several similar instances or events). Also: case (a legal proceeding, lawsuit)
seznam — list (register of items). Also: list
slovo — word (distinct unit of language). Also: word (promise)
strana — side. Also: page (of a book), party (in negotiation and litigation), party
tělo — body (physical structure of a living human or animal). Also: body (corpse), body (torso), body (largest or most important part of anything), body

být — to be. Also: to be (to exist), used to form past tense forms of verbs with past participles, used to form future tense forms of verbs with infinitives, used to form conditional mood forms of verbs with past participles, used to form passive voice forms of verbs with passive participles, used to form conditional forms of verbs with past and passive participles
dělat — to do. Also: to make, to pretend, to feign
ještě — still. Also: yet, even (emphasising a comparative), alright

místo — place (location, position). Also: place (seat), space (occupied by or intended for a person or thing), place (as in decimal place), job, employment, locus (a place or locality, especially a centre of activity or the scene of a crime)
tam — there (in or at that place or location). Also: there (to or into that place)
český — Czech. Also: Bohemian (of, or relating to Bohemia)

dům — house (building, structure intended for housing). Also: house (an institution that provides certain services or serves various purposes and activities), home (a place where someone lives, resides, is at home, has their own household, family, where they feel safe), house (royal or noble family), house (sector of the horoscope representing a certain area of a person's life)
malý — small. Also: minor (the smaller of two intervals denoted by the same ordinal number)
název — title (of a book or other media). Also: name (of a chemical element)
ostatní — other (than the one previously referred to). Also: else
skupina — group. Also: troop (group of people), moiety (a specific segment of a molecule)
soud — court (organ of justice). Also: judgment
udělat — to do. Also: to make, to harm, injure, to become, turn (of weather), to capsize, to ejaculate semen, cum
věc — thing. Also: business (something involving one personally), subject (of a mail), thing, entity (thing, living or non-living, real or unreal)
výkon — power (the rate of doing work). Also: performance
zápas — match (sporting event). Also: struggle, wrestling (sport)
země — earth. Also: ground (surface of the Earth), country, land (state)
lepší — better, comparative degree of dobrý.
Praha — Prague (the capital city of the Czech Republic).
prostě — simply (in a simple manner). Also: just, simply
bývalý — former (such that used to be but is no more).
centrum — centre (of a city). Also: centre (place where activity occurs)
číslo — number. Also: issue (issue of a magazine)
dostat — to get (to receive). Also: to get somewhere
náměstí — square (open space in a town).
období — period (length of time). Also: period (epoch)
Plzeň — Pilsen (a city in the Czech Republic).
stav — stance. Also: state (condition), state, loom (frame)
Tomáš — Thomas (biblical character). Also: a male given name, equivalent to English Thomas
mít — to have. Also: to be (to be doing fine or poorly), to measure, to be obliged to, to be supposed to
stejně — equally (in equal degree or extent). Also: anyway, all the same, in the same way
článek — article (text piece). Also: paper, article (scientific), cell (of an electrical battery), link (element of a chain)
dále — farther, comparative degree of daleko.
dát — to give. Also: to put, to start, to begin, to let, to allow, to have (partake of a particular substance)
hrát — to play (a game). Also: to play (a role), to play (a musical instrument), to use a toy
menší — smaller, comparative degree of malý.
mít na sobě — to wear (to have on (clothes)).
mít rád — to like (to enjoy). Also: to love
společnost — society (people of one’s country or community as a whole). Also: company, corporation, company (social visitors)
zároveň — at once, at the same time, simultaneously.
zvláštní — special. Also: strange, peculiar, curious, particular
vlastně — actually, as a matter of fact.
funkce — function. Also: function (math: a relation between a set of inputs and a set of permissible outputs), function (programming: routine that returns a result), feature
jít po — go after, to pursue someone in an attempt to get them. Also: go after, to pursue an object or a goal, Used other than figuratively or idiomatically: see jít, po.
nechat — to leave (to not take away with oneself but leave as available for others). Also: to leave (to transfer responsibility or attention), to let, allow, to keep
společně — together (at the same time, in the same place, in close association).
stát — to stand. Also: to stay on place, to stand by, to hold by, to cost, to be worth
tisíc — thousand. Also: a thousand crowns (Czech currency)
zájem — interest (attention). Also: interest (an involvement, claim, right, share, stake in or link with a financial, business, or other undertaking or endeavor)
daleko — far, afar (long distance, physical or temporal). Also: much, by far
postupně — gradually (in gradual manner). Also: step by step, one by one
včera — yesterday (on the day before today).
hra — play, game. Also: game, play (theatrical play)
navíc — extra, odd. Also: in addition, on top of that, moreover, what's more
teprve — only, just (not yet more than).
vláda — government (political body). Also: rule, reign, control
jít — to walk, go (on foot). Also: to work, do, suffice, can, to be possible, to concern, matter, interest (to be at issue, to be at stake)
mít pravdu — to be right, to be correct.
možnost — possibility (property of being possible). Also: option, possibility, choice, alternative
pracovní den — working day (day of a week in which work is done).
škoda — pity, shame. Also: harm, damage
způsob — way, manner (method). Also: mood, grammatical mood
nejlepší — best, finest, superlative degree of dobrý.
abych byl — for me to be.
okolí — surroundings, surrounding area. Also: neighborhood
právě — just, exactly. Also: just now
pro a proti — pros and cons.
kolo — bicycle, bike. Also: wheel, round
počet — count, number (quantity). Also: calculus (formal system for symbol manipulation), computation
zrovna — just, exactly. Also: right now, just now
už — again, already. Also: yet, anymore, any more (any longer)
smysl — sense, meaning. Also: purpose, meaning, point, sense (sight, smell, etc.)
asi — I guess/I think. Also: approximately
jednou za čas — once in a while.
najednou — all of a sudden. Also: at once, at the same time, simultaneously, together
věc o sobě — thing-in-itself, thing in itself.
velice — very, very much. Also: greatly
zatím — in the meantime. Also: yet, so far, meanwhile
proto — therefore, hence, that is why.
množství — quantity, amount. Also: abundance, wealth
noc — night, nighttime (period of time from sundown to sunup).
přitom — at the same time. Also: yet
dlouho — long, a long time. Also: long, a particular amount of time (determined in the sentence)
no — certainly, indeed, of course. Also: yeah, yep
sám — alone, by oneself. Also: lonely
třeba — necessary, needed. Also: maybe, for instance, say
čas od času — from time to time.
udělat něco navíc — go the extra mile.
přes tu chvíli — every now and then.
základní — basic, fundamental, underlying. Also: basal (related to base), cardinal
Petr — a male given name, equivalent to English Peter. Also: Peter (the Apostle)
podobně — similarly, likewise (in a similar manner).
přece — indicates annoyance, forcing agreement with the statement which it modifies. Also: yet, in spite of that
vzhledem — given, due, because, owing. Also: in view, considering
stále — always, all the time. Also: still (up to the time, no less than before)
původní — original, indigenous, native. Also: original, novel, creative
celkem — quite, pretty, fairly (to a moderate extent). Also: in total
pracovat — to work, to have a job. Also: to work
hned — immediately, right away. Also: right, exactly
Česko — Czech Republic, Czechia (a country in Central Europe; official name: Česká republika). Also: the Czech lands, Bohemia, one of the historical regions of the Czech Republic
občas — sometimes, occasionally, from time to time.
přesto — yet, still, nevertheless, in spite of that.
vlastní jméno — proper noun, proper name.
zdarma — free of charge, for free.
získat — to gain, obtain, to earn, to win.

ČR — initialism of Čečenská republika (“the Chechen Republic”). Also: initialism of Čuvašská republika (“the Chuvash Republic”), initialism of Česká republika (“the Czech Republic, CR”), initialism of Čínská republika (“the Republic of China, ROC”)
v. o. s. — initialism of veřejná obchodní společnost.
starší — member of a municipal legislative body, alderman. Also: layman leading an evangelic community, elder, guild master, leader of a community
ODS — acronym of Občanská demokratická strana, Czech political party.

rok — year, the time it takes a planetary body to complete one revolution around a star. Also: year, exactly 365.25 days, year, a period between set dates that denotes a year, year, a scheduled part of a year spent in a given activity
vždyť — indicates a slight annoyance that the other speaker took so long to agree. Also: tags a sentence to contradict or counteract the preceding sentence by another person
nahoře — up (in a higher position). Also: up (on top of something), on top, up (in a good mood)
pán — Mr. Also: man, gentleman, master (someone who has control over something or someone), master (owner of an animal or slave), lord
vzhůru — up (to a higher position). Also: up, awake
běh — run (act of running). Also: running

čaj — tea (the dried leaves or buds of the tea plant, Camellia sinensis). Also: tea (the drink made by infusing these dried leaves or buds in hot water), tea (a variety of the tea plant), tea (by extension, any drink made by infusing parts of various other plants)
denní — day. Also: daily, diurnal (occurring every day)
doba — age (particular period of time in history). Also: time, period, beat
Eva — Eve. Also: a female given name
klíč — key (of a lock). Also: wrench (US), spanner (UK), clef, key, key (field of a relation constrained to be unique), clue (informations which can lead one to a certain point)
kluk — boy. Also: boyfriend, jack (playing card)
kočka — cat (domestic animal). Also: attractive woman
Lev — Leo (male given name). Also: a male surname transferred from the given name
mapa — map (visual representation of an area).
oko — eye. Also: twenty-one, pontoon, tarn, eye (center of a storm)
pes — dog. Also: male dog, scoundrel, bad person
plyn — gas (state of matter). Also: gas (fuel), gas pedal
prdel — ass (buttocks). Also: fun, backwater (remote place)
starý — old (of an object). Also: old (of a being)
umět — can (to be able). Also: to speak, to understand (object: language)
věk — age (duration of an entity). Also: age (particular period of time in history), century
brod — ford (location where a stream is shallow).
dílo — work (literary, artistic, or intellectual production).
dlouhý — long. Also: tall (when talking about persons)
dole — down (at a lower place or position).
dřevo — wood (substance). Also: wood (a type of golf club)
duše — soul (spirit). Also: inner tube, sound post
gól — goal (action in sports). Also: turn up for the book (usually a part of the phrase To je (teda) gól.)
hlava — head (part of the body). Also: head (end of a nail), head (side of a coin), chapter (section of a text)
jednotka — unit (standard measure of a quantity). Also: unit (organized group of people or things)
klid — rest (absence of motion). Also: rest, calm
klub — club (association of members). Also: club (establishment providing entertainment, nightclub)
kniha — book (a collection of sheets of paper bound together to hinge at one edge). Also: book (a major division of a published work), omasum (the third portion in the stomach of a ruminant)
král — king (male ruler). Also: king (chess figure), king (playing card)
kultura — arts. Also: culture (arts, customs and habits), culture
láska — love (intense feeling of affection and care toward another person). Also: love (object of one’s romantic feelings)
linka — line. Also: phone line, counter, route, line (itinerary of stops of mass transportation)
list — leaf (green and flat organ of vegetative plants). Also: letter (written message), sheet (sheet of paper), newspaper, certificate (document containing a certified statement)
masa — mass (a large body of individuals, especially persons).
míč — ball (sports). Also: million crowns (million units of Czech currency)
místnost — room (separate part of a building, enclosed by walls, a floor and a ceiling).

questionTemplates.questionFormat: Which word is closest in meaning to '{targetLanguageWord}'?
